name: Fionn mac Cumhaill Extended
description: Fionn mac Cumhaill, also known as Finn McCool, is a prominent hero in Irish mythology, renowned for his wisdom, bravery, and leadership of the Fianna, a band of elite warriors. Born as Demne, he gained the name Fionn ('fair' or 'white') after burning his thumb while cooking the Salmon of Knowledge. This act granted him immense wisdom, allowing him to foresee the future and understand the secrets of the world. He is often depicted as a giant, shaping the Irish landscape with his legendary feats, such as creating the Giant's Causeway. His adventures involve battles against monsters, interactions with otherworldly beings, and the pursuit of love and justice. Fionn's tales are integral to the Fenian Cycle, a collection of stories that celebrate the heroism and chivalry of the Fianna. He is a symbol of Irish strength, knowledge, and the enduring spirit of folklore. His legacy continues to inspire and shape Irish identity, embodying the ideals of a just and powerful leader. He is also known for his encounters with the Tuatha Dé Danann and his role in maintaining the balance between the mortal and supernatural realms. Fionn's disappearance is shrouded in mystery, with some legends claiming he sleeps beneath Ireland, awaiting the time of his country's greatest need.
subtitle: Wisdom, bravery, and leadership of the Fianna
achievements: Gaining the Wisdom of the Salmon

keyMyths.title: The Salmon of Knowledge
keyMyths.description: Fionn accidentally gains immense wisdom by burning his thumb while cooking the Salmon of Knowledge for the druid Finegas and instinctively sucking on it. This grants him the ability to access all the knowledge of the world.

keyMyths.title: Defeating Aillen mac Midgna
keyMyths.description: Fionn uses a spear given to him by his father's sister to kill Aillen mac Midgna, who had been burning Tara every Samhain. This act establishes him as a great warrior and protector of Ireland.

keyMyths.title: The Pursuit of Diarmuid and Gráinne
keyMyths.description: Fionn pursues Diarmuid Ua Duibhne and Gráinne after Gráinne is betrothed to him but elopes with Diarmuid. This tale highlights themes of love, betrayal, and the complexities of honor.
keyMyths.source: The Pursuit of Diarmuid and Gráinne

sources.author: Lady Gregory
sources.description: A collection of Irish myths and legends, including stories of Fionn mac Cumhaill.
sources.text: A collection of Irish myths and legends, including stories of Fionn mac Cumhaill.

sources.author: James Macpherson
sources.description: A controversial but influential collection of poems attributed to Fionn's son, Ossian.
